coredata.dc:title: Direct recycling of graphite from spent batteries and production scraps for the development of a circular and sustainable economy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Muguruza-Sánchez, Ane
Sananes-Israel, Susan
Moliner, Enrique
Contreras, Edgar
Landa-Medrano, Imanol
Palomares, Verónica
de Meatza, Iratxe
coredata.dc:description: Current lithium-ion battery recycling processes are based on high-temperature calcination (pyrometallurgy) or leaching treatments (hydrometallurgy), requiring huge amounts of energy and producing considerable waste. Direct recycling protocols are based on the reconstruction and regeneration of materials, eliminating the need for further material processing. In this paper, graphite electrodes have been recycled via a direct recycling protocol based on mild leaching with H2SO4 and H2O2 and calcination to eliminate the impurities and regenerate the structure. A Design of Experiments (DOE) has been proposed to determine the leaching conditions that reduce the generated waste and environmental impact, for which a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) has been carried out. This combination of experimental and analytical methods has been useful to determine the parameters that have the greatest impact on the environment and select the most sustainable leaching condition, which, in this case, has shown a reduction of 36 % in acidification and 14 % in water use. The established recycling route has been validated with graphite anodes from production scraps and cycled cells (End-of-Life condition, EoL, SOH%<80 %), and in both cases, the polymeric compounds used in the electrode slurry preparation have been eliminated and the graphitization degree has been restored. These results show that graphite can be recycled from LIBs to develop a direct recycling route that promotes a sustainable circular economy and diminishes material waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Direct recycling
Life cycle assessment
Graphite
Battery recycling
Circular economy
LIB

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by CIDETEC and the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme [No. 101069865 (RESPECT project)]. V. P. acknowledges the support of Gobierno Vasco/Eusko Jaurlaritza (project IT1546-22 ). The TGA characterizations were performed in Laboratorio Naturklima/Naturklima Laborategia from CIDETEC, funded by Naturklima and Diputaci\u00F3n Foral de Gipuzkoa .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the direct recycling process used for electrodes from the production scrap and from EoL electrodes. (b) Production scraps and (c) EoL electrode used for the direct recycling process.

FE-SEM images of the production scrap electrodes (a) before and (b) after leaching-calcination (LC), (c) after calcination-leaching (CL); (d) TGA results of the production scrap electrode LC and (e) CL before the recycling, after the first recycling step and after the whole process.

(a)TGA results of the graphite samples recycled following conditions #5 and (b) #8. The red line in the Deriv. Weight (% °C-1) Y axis represents the untreated production scrap sample to use as a reference for the SBR and CMC peaks. The peak corresponding to SBR, at around 450–500 °C, cannot be observed in the samples, and the signal corresponding to CMC, at around 250–350 °C, is observed because the TGA measurements were done before the calcination. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Environmental impact variation of direct recycling process for different processing conditions compared to #ref, including acidification (AC), climate change (CC), freshwater ecotoxicity (ECF), particulate matter (PM), marine eutrophication (EUM), freshwater eutrophication (EUF), terrestrial eutrophication (EUT) carcinogenic human toxicity (HTC), non-carcinogenic human toxicity (HTNC), ionising radiation (IR), land use (LU), ozone depletion (OD), photochemical ozone formation (POF), fossil resource use (RUF), mineral and metal resource use (RUMM), and water use (WU).
Climate change impact of the direct recycling process for different processing conditions. Contribution of each parameter in the different methods is also shown.

Schematic summary of the selected direct recycling process for graphite anodes, based on the DOE and LCA results. A mild calcination is followed by a leaching step with a mild acid leaching to eliminate the polymeric components present in the electrodes.

TGA curve of the recycled graphite materials (LC, in red, and CL, in green) and the pristine electrode. CMC decomposition temperature: 250–300 °C; SBR evaporation temperature: 450–500 °C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Production scraps after the calcination step in the furnace at 400 °C. (b) TGA results of recycled graphite from production scrap and cycled cells. (c) XRD diagrams of the samples with the characteristic graphite peaks marked to compare the crystallinity of the recycled materials: (002) at 26.5°, (100) peak at 42°, (101) peak at 45°, (004) peak at 54.5° and (110) peak at 77.5°.

Electrochemical results obtained with lab-scale electrodes with 25 % recycled graphites: Gr.LC, Gr.CL and GrC.CL, compared to a pristine graphite anode. Half-coin cells were assembled with lithium metal counter electrode and the forming cycle at C/20 was analysed for the electrochemical validation.
Experimental conditions for the Design of Experiments proposed. Each #condition was repeated three times, and TGA measurements were done on all the samples.

Results obtained from the experiments carried out following the conditions of the DOE. The atomic% of Cu was determined by EDX measurements and the loss of SBR was obtained by integrating the peaks in the TGA curves.

Leaching conditions that obtained graphite material that met the criteria set to be suitable for reprocessing, and values of the reference leaching protocol from the first DOE (presented in Table S1).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: Battery Recycling published in Journal of Power Sources Advances.

head.ce:title: Direct recycling of graphite from spent batteries and production scraps for the development of a circular and sustainable economy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CIDETEC, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA), Po. Miramón 196, 20014, Donostia-San Sebastián, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CIDETEC, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA), Po. Miramon 196, 20014, Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic and Inorganic Chemistry Department, University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU), 48940, Bilbao, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Organic and Inorganic Chemistry department, University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU), 48940 Bilbao, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LOMARTOV S.L., C/Alfareria 3, 46100, Burjassot, Valencia, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CIDETEC, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA), Po. Miramón 196, 20014, Donostia-San Sebastián, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current lithium-ion battery recycling processes are based on high-temperature calcination (pyrometallurgy) or leaching treatments (hydrometallurgy), requiring huge amounts of energy and producing considerable waste. Direct recycling protocols are based on the reconstruction and regeneration of materials, eliminating the need for further material processing. In this paper, graphite electrodes have been recycled via a direct recycling protocol based on mild leaching with H2SO4 and H2O2 and calcination to eliminate the impurities and regenerate the structure. A Design of Experiments (DOE) has been proposed to determine the leaching conditions that reduce the generated waste and environmental impact, for which a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) has been carried out. This combination of experimental and analytical methods has been useful to determine the parameters that have the greatest impact on the environment and select the most sustainable leaching condition, which, in this case, has shown a reduction of 36 % in acidification and 14 % in water use. The established recycling route has been validated with graphite anodes from production scraps and cycled cells (End-of-Life condition, EoL, SOH%<80 %), and in both cases, the polymeric compounds used in the electrode slurry preparation have been eliminated and the graphitization degree has been restored. These results show that graphite can be recycled from LIBs to develop a direct recycling route that promotes a sustainable circular economy and diminishes material waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The acute increase in the demand for Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIB) in the last years, especially due to an expansion in the use of electric vehicles, requires urgent measures to decrease the dependency of Europe on the export of raw battery materials from Asia and avoid the landfill of spent batteries. The constant increase in the demand for these batteries, which is rising yearly by almost 20 % and has been estimated to reach 27 million units in 2030 [1,2], is also generating huge battery waste that is not being correctly disposed of. Most batteries that have reached their End-of-Life (80 % of the initial capacity, EoL) are discarded in landfills, which can create serious environmental pollution by leaking toxic components into the soil and water and producing fires and explosions due to the degradation of the components [3–9].

Graphite constitutes 12–21 wt% of a battery and considering that 1 kg of graphite is required to obtain 1 kWh of capacity [10,11]. Due to this high demand, natural graphite has been included in the critical raw materials list, as its excessive extraction will pose a severe threat to future supply. Therefore, taking measures to ensure availability of natural graphite is imperative [12]. The recycling of graphite from spent batteries has not been deeply studied up till now, as the focus has been placed on high-valuable metals such as Ni, Co and Li from the cathode and electrolyte [2]. However, a proper method for reusing the graphite from spent batteries would alleviate the shortage of resources and bring considerable economic benefits [5,13,14].

Currently, the most common recycling methods are pyrometallurgy and hydrometallurgy [6,7,15]. In the former, batteries are crushed and smelted at elevated temperatures to recover only specific elements, such as Co, Ni, or Li [2,11,16]. In the latter, batteries are crushed and leached to recover salts after precipitation, filtration, and purification processes. In both recycling methods, the materials are recovered in the form of salts, which need to be reprocessed to obtain the battery-grade components, and graphite is usually not recovered [2,16]. Considering that graphite residues maintain their structural integrity and do not require further processing to be reinserted into the electrode manufacturing loop, recycling of spent graphite seems a suitable alternative to mining and high-temperature calcination of newly produced natural and artificial graphite [8,10,12].

New direct recycling processes of battery materials are emerging, as an alternative to traditional pyrometallurgical or hydrometallurgical routes, because they allow the recovery of the graphite with restored structural integrity and electrochemical properties of pristine materials with minimal treatment and lower environmental impact [11,17,18]. Direct recycling methods consist on the recovery of battery-grade materials with little or no additional modification, involving multiple physical and chemical processes with low temperature and mild acids to recover regenerated structures that can be directly used in the preparation of electrodes, creating a closed loop. The currently established standard procedure includes mild acid leaching to eliminate the polymers used in the electrode processing and mild calcination to regenerate the structural order of the graphite. Different acid combinations or calcination procedures can be applied that yield regenerated materials with high efficiency. Yang et al. [1] performed the calcination step at 500 °C under Ar atmosphere and leached the graphite using both deionized water and 3 M HCl to determine the most suitable route to recover a regenerated graphite to be reused in electrode production. Zhang et al. [5] leached the graphite in a mixture of H2SO4 and H2O2 before the calcination in air to obtain a heat-treated anode material that was coated with phenolic resin to obtain a regenerated graphite that exceeded the electrochemical performance of pristine graphite. The combination of H2SO4 and H2O2 has proven to increase the interlayer distance between the graphene sheets, decreasing the graphitization degree. To compensate for this modification of the structure, the calcination step is essential to regenerate the crystallite height and amplitude, which determines the graphitization degree of graphite. Lc (crystallite height) estimates the number of graphene layers stacked in the c axis of the crystallite, while La (lateral length of crystallite) refers to the amplitude of said layers [19].

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a comprehensive and internationally standardized methodology —outlined in ISO 14040/14044— to analyse the environmental impact of products, processes and systems [20–22]. It is considered ideal for investigating the environmental repercussion of recycling processes, as it offers a standardized and structured framework that prompts critical analysis on the environmental profile of recycling, helping to prevent unintended secondary pollution [23,24]. Indeed, LCA has been widely used to evaluate the environmental impacts associated with LIB recycling. Domingues and de Souza [24] identified and reviewed 64 LCA studies on LIB recycling, including hydrometallurgical, pyrometallurgical, direct recycling, and bioleaching processes. Electricity consumption and chemical inputs were identified as environmental hotspots for all recycling options. The authors concluded that LCA can be a powerful tool to guide the development and implementation of more sustainable recycling technologies for managing and recovering valuable materials from LIBs, although they highlighted some limitations in the current state of the art. Unlike hydrometallurgical and pyrometallurgical recycling, which are the most evaluated routes in LCA studies, direct recycling has few evaluations [25–30]. The main target materials of LIBs recycling considered in the LCA studies are metals (Co, Li, Ni, Mn, Cu), with few studies including electrolyte or graphite recycling. Furthermore, existing studies focus on the climate change impact category, representing simplified LCA, which results do not provide a holistic view of the environmental performance of LIB recycling options, since other impact categories are often neglected, which can omit serious secondary pollution risks. Particularly for direct recycling, impact values were found only for two categories, namely climate change and water use. Hence, comprehensive impact assessments, with good coverage of impacts, for direct recycling are lacking in the literature.

Considering the present and predicted raw material shortage of graphite and the environmental impact of the currently established recycling methods, a direct recycling protocol has been proposed to create a sustainable closed-loop graphite supply. Different sized batches of graphite anodes have been recycled ranging from 10 g up to 65 g. The recovered graphite has been characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). After initial trials with State-of-the-Art leaching and calcination conditions, a Design of Experiments (DOE) has been used, coupled with an LCA study, to determine the leaching parameters that are more crucial to reduce the environmental impact of the whole direct recycling process. The final recycling process shows promising results, with production scraps and cycled cells, successfully proving its usability also when reversing the steps.

Production scrap electrodes were directly obtained from the CIDETEC pilot R2R coating line, with a water-based formulation of 94 % graphite (Gr, active material), 2 % carbon black (CB), 2 % sodium carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), and 2 % styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) polymer binder; the electrodes were casted onto battery grade 8 μm-thick copper foil (Furukawa). For the EoL electrodes, 1 Ah pouch cells consisting of LiNi0.6Co0.2Mn0.2O2 (NMC622) cathode and Gr anode previously subjected to different cycling conditions were fully discharged in a battery tester and opened in a glove box, with water and oxygen content <0.1 ppm. The graphite electrodes were not washed with any solvent to preserve the lithium salts and SEI that had been deposited, to mimic industrial-scale recycling conditions and had the same formulation as the production scraps to ensure a fair comparison.

The direct recycling method selected in this experiment consisted of a mild leaching with a mixture of sulfuric acid solution (H2SO4) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as the oxidizing agent to dissolve the copper from the current collector and the SBR, followed by a calcination step at 400 °C to eliminate the CMC co-binder and purify the structure of the graphite.

For the leaching step, 10 g of production scraps were plunged into a 2 M H2SO4 solution mixed with 5 % H2O2 in a beaker [8,12,31]. The production scraps (Fig. 1 (b)) were mechanically stirred for 2 h at 70 °C, after which the solid content was filtered and dried in a convection oven at 60 °C for at least 24 h, obtaining the leached graphite (Gr.L). The calcination was carried out in a chamber furnace heated at a 5 °C min−1 rate until 400 °C were reached, where it was maintained for 1 h, in air atmosphere [1,5,14,32]; the recycled graphite material was recovered (Gr.LC). The same protocol was performed in the reverse order with production scraps to compare the effect that the order of the steps have on the recovered material, Gr. CL. Fig. 1(a) shows the schematic illustration of the direct recycling process that was followed.

With the aim of reducing the waste generated during the leaching step and increasing the efficiency, a Design of Experiments (DOE) was proposed. A DOE is an analysis methodology based on a certain number of experiments conducted with multiple input parameters being purposefully varied to analyse the results and identify important interactions [33]. An orthogonal matrix was selected to analyse the whole parameter space with minimum experimental trials, reducing the experimental time required by conventional methods. The selection of the appropriate Taguchi design was based on the number of parameters and levels that match the requirements. For this experiment four parameters were selected at two levels, therefore, Taguchi L8 (24) design was selected to determine the most appropriate condition combination [34]. The criteria to corroborate the suitability of a leaching condition was based on the elimination of the metallic copper from the current collector by visual inspection of the recovered material and EDX measurements and the elimination of the SBR by Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Thus, the outputs were not suitable for a statistical analysis (yes/no responses).

The work performed by Alcaraz et al. [12] was used as a reference for the DOE on the leaching step after it was validated with production scraps. The leaching process consisted on stirring the electrodes in a dissolution of 2 M H2SO4 + 5 vol% H2O2, at 70 °C for 2 h with mechanical mixing (52 g L−1 solid-to-liquid ratio). The four parameters that were varied during the DOE were the solid-to-liquid ratio, leaching temperature and time, and concentration of the sulfuric acid. Each condition was tested three times to ensure repeatability between the tests. The overview of the experimental conditions of each experiment is shown in Table 1 .

For a DOE to be advantageous, different results should be obtained to correlate different conditions and interactions and select the most suitable combination. A previous DOE with slighter condition variations yielded the same results across all conditions (Table S1). Therefore, a definitive DOE set of experiments was outlined by using the most moderate conditions of the initial DOE as a reference to set the baseline and widening the variation in the analysed parameters (Table 1).

LCA methodology was used to calculate and compare the potential environmental impacts of the direct recycling process under the different processing conditions tested to settle on the most sustainable combination of parameters. LCA is the most widely accepted methodology to evaluate the environmental burdens of a defined system, either a product or a process. In brief, LCA involves a material and energy balance applied to the product's system, combined with an assessment of the environmental impacts related to the input and outputs to and from the product system.

The functional unit selected for this analysis was 1 kg of metals recovered through the direct recycling process, allowing to compare the environmental impacts related to different processing conditions on a common basis. The system boundaries included the upstream production of the electricity, water, and chemicals (H2O2 and H2SO4) consumed by the process, whose inventories were taken from the Ecoivent database [35]. Electricity demand was estimated based on the technical specifications of a stirred reactor with a heated jacket and considering the processing time and temperature of each experiment. The Environmental Footprint 3.1 method [36], as integrated into the SimaPro software, was applied to convert the inventory data related to the process inputs and output into their corresponding potential environmental impacts, which are classified into sixteen impact categories like climate change (a.k.a. carbon footprint) or use of fossil, mineral, and metal resources, among others.
Thermogravimetric analysis. TGA was conducted using a Thermogravimetric Analyser (Q500, TA Instruments) in air with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

X-ray diffraction. XRD tests were performed on a Bruker D8 Advanced diffractometer with Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) equipped with a LynxEye PSD detector. The diffractograms were recorded between 2θ = 10° and 80° at 0.019° s−1, and fitted using the FULLPROF program [37].

Microscopy. The morphology of the active materials was analysed by means of field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, ULTRA plus ZEISS). The samples were prepared in the lab and stuck to the holder using carbon adhesive tape. FE-SEM micrographs at different magnifications (from × 200 to × 10000) were obtained.

The electrodes were fabricated by mixing 94 wt% active material (25 % recycled graphite and 75 % pristine graphite GHDR 15-4, IMERYS Carbon & Graphite), 2 wt% (Super C45, IMERYS Carbon & Graphite), 2 wt% carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, T2000 PA, DOW), and 2 wt% stryrene butadiene rubber (SBR, BM451B, ZEON) in the DI water with a loading level of 8.5 ± 1 mg/cm2 (2.8 mAh/cm2 based on a specific capacity of 350 mAh/g) in a Speed Mixer (DAC 700.2 VAC-P, Hauschild). The resulting slurry was coated in a doctor Blade with a wet gap of and dried in a convection oven at 60 °C during 20 min. Electrodes were calendered to 1.45 g/cm3 density.

Disks of 16.6 mm diameter were cut and dried overnight at 120 °C under vacuum in a dry room to eliminate the water traces (dew point of −50 °C). Coin cells CR2025 (Hohsen) were assembled using lithium metal as counter electrode (Ø = 18.2 mm, 50 μm thickness, from Albemarle). A glass-fibre separator (Whatman GF/A) separator was used and 100 μL of 1 M LiPF6 solution in a mixture of ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate (1:1 in volume) with 2 wt% vinylidene carbonate was the selected electrolyte. After assembly, the coin cells were tested in a Basytec Cell Test System potentiostat at 25 °C ± 1 °C controlled by air conditioning.

Cells were kept at open circuit for 16 h to ensure the wetting of the separator and the electrode pores with the electrolyte. The validation electrochemical experiments consisted of a cycle at 0.05C (CCCV lithiation until 0.025C, CC delithiation) in the potential window of 0.01–1 V vs. Li/Li+. A specific capacity of 350 mAh·ggraphite −1 was defined for the calculation of the C-rate.

The reference direct recycling protocol was tested with production scraps by performing leaching-calcination (LC) and calcination-leaching (CL) steps to verify the validity of the method. After the material was filtered following the leaching step, no copper residues were observed in the material, and EDX measurements further confirmed the elimination (not shown); FE-SEM images showed that graphite from untreated production scraps exhibited sharp edges with flaky particles (Fig. 2 (a)). However, graphite recycled from production scraps, both with LC and CL protocol (Fig. 2(b) and (c), respectively) presented more spheric shapes and rounder edges, similar to pristine graphite (Fig. S1). Graphite particles in the recycled samples were covered with smaller particles that were identified as carbon black, the conductive agent, which was not targeted for removal in the selected direct recycling protocol. TGA analyses were performed to corroborate the elimination of the polymeric binders, which should occur at 450–500 °C for SBR and at 250–350 °C for CMC. Values obtained below the 0.01 % derivative weight threshold were not considered to be present in the sample.

For the direct recycling procedure carried out with production scraps leaching-calcination (LC), the results are shown in Fig. 2(d), while calcination-leaching (CL) results are displayed in Fig. 2(e). The SBR was completely eliminated after the leaching step, and the peak corresponding to the CMC that could be observed in the untreated sample disappeared after the calcination was performed, both for CL and LC samples. The light blue curve, representing recycled graphite obtained after both steps, presented a steady line on the Weight% axis, indicating that no mass was lost during the temperature rise, evidencing no decomposition of polymeric materials or any type of residues.

Each experiment performed in the DOE, in which the solid-to-liquid ratio, acid concentration, leaching temperature and time were modified, was validated by visually confirming the elimination of the current collector, which was also proved by EDX measurements (Fig. S2–S3) and TGA analyses to corroborate the elimination of the polymeric binders. The calculation of remaining polymeric binders was done by integrating the area between 450°C and 550 °C corresponding to the signal of SBR [5] The results obtained in the DOE, shown in Table 2 allowed the determination of the most suitable parameter combinations for an optimized leaching process.

The LCA results are summarized in Fig. 4 , which illustrates how the environmental impacts of the recycling process varied depending on the processing conditions (from #1 to #8) compared to the reference process (#ref). Processing conditions #1–3 showed significant increase in most environmental impact categories, such as a 115–133 % increase for climate change or a 120–146 % increase for mineral and metal resource use. Conditions #6 and #7 also consistently increased impacts, up to 70 % for climate change and 80 % for mineral and metal resource use. In contrast, #8 showed an environmental profile comparable to the reference conditions, achieving limited reductions in some impact categories and slight increases in others. Among the evaluated processing conditions, only #4 and #5 stood out, showing environmental improvements, that is, a decrease in the calculated impacts across all categories. These two processing conditions consistently reduced the footprints, highlighting their environmental viability as optimized alternatives to the reference processing conditions. The greatest reductions were observed for condition #4, especially in acidification (reduced by 80 %), particulate matter (79 %), non-carcinogenic human toxicity (37 %), and photochemical ozone formation (34 %), while for condition #5 the reductions were smaller yet significant, e.g. 36 % reduction for acidification, 35 % for particulate matter, 31 % for ionising radiation or 28 % for fossil resource use.

Fig. 5 displays the climate change impact of the direct recycling process under the different processing conditions (#1–8 and #ref), showing the impact share of the electricity, water, and chemicals consumed by the process. It can be observed again that only processing conditions #4 and #5 resulted in a carbon footprint reduction compared to the reference condition (#ref), although the impact reduction was quite limited in this case (5 % and 8 %, respectively). The carbon footprint for conditions #4 and #5 was estimated at 2.99 and 2.88 kg of CO2 equivalent per kilogram of metals recovered, respectively, which was slightly lower than for #ref (3.13 kg CO2 eq kg−1). The carbon footprint was driven mainly by the usage of hydrogen peroxide, which caused over 90 % of the total footprint in all processing conditions tested (#1–8). Specifically, for conditions #4 and #5, hydrogen peroxide comprised 96 % and 92 % of the carbon footprint, while the impact contribution from electricity, water, and sulfuric acid was comparatively negligible. This dominant trend of hydrogen peroxide was confirmed in most of the remaining impact categories, although the contributions of other inputs could be more prominent depending on the impact category. The only exceptions were found in acidification and land use impacts, where the main environmental hotspot was the usage of sulfuric acid.

At this point, it is important to note that the LCA was based on laboratory-scale data. While an LCA can be developed using mass balance and energy consumption data obtained from laboratory tests, the results will not be comparable to industrial-scale processing. Beyond the obvious difference in scale, laboratory production is typically performed as a non-optimized batch process, often leading to high energy consumption for start-up and shutdown, as well as potential product wastage due to equipment cleaning. Comparative LCAs performed on the same process at different scales revealed significant disparities between the industrial-scale LCA and that generated using lab-scale data alone [38]. Hence, the aim of the present LCA study was limited to the preliminary evaluation of the environmental viability of the processing conditions tested, which was found positive for #4 and #5. It was also useful to identify the main process hotspots, given in this case by the hydrogen peroxide usage, highlighting the need to reduce the consumption of this reagent (or even replacing it), which is expected to be achieved as the process is scaled up by the optimization of reagents usage and their possible recovery and reuse.

Having gathered and analysed all the data regarding experimental results and LCA analysis, it was considered that #5 was the most appropriate condition to be used in the leaching step of this direct recycling process. Therefore, all the results shown from this point onwards refer to graphite obtained from the recycling of materials using the leaching conditions #5: 1.2 M H2SO4 + 5 vol% H2O2, 65 g L−1, 40 °C leaching for 20 min (Fig. 6 ). The direct recycling protocol #5 was validated with production scraps following the leaching-calcination (Gr.LC) and calcination-leaching (Gr.CL) route, to prove that the process was suitable in both ways. TGA results of Gr. LC and Gr. CL are shown in Fig. 7 . After the calcination and leaching steps, the SBR binder and CMC dispersant were eliminated from both materials, with a recovery yield of >85 % for the graphite-carbon black mixture (considering the current collector and the mass fractions of SBR and CMC from the original electrode).

XRD measurements were also performed in these samples to further study the effect that the order of the processes may have on the final material (Fig. 8 (c)). The diffractograms were fitted using the P63/mmc hexagonal space group (JCPDS, Card No. 75–1621/41–1487, graphite) [3,41]. The characteristic graphite peak at 26.5° 2θ, corresponding to the (002) plane, was observed in all samples, with higher intensity on Gr. CL, concluding that, by performing the calcination in the first place, a recycled graphite material with higher structural order was obtained. This same peak was slightly shifted to lower angles in the recycled samples, which suggests an increase in the interlayer distance, probably caused by the insertion of the leachate in the leaching process [31]. The unit cell parameters, graphitization degree, and fitted diffractograms to compare the graphite samples recycled with different procedure orders can be found in Table S2 and Fig. S4. Pattern matching function of FullProf program was used to refine the unit cell parameters of the obtained different graphite samples. Crystallite height and width of single graphite particles were calculated by applying the Scherrer Equations (Equation S(1) and Equation S(2)) to (002) and (100) crystal plane peak positions [19]. As confirmed by the intensity of the 26.5° peak, the graphitization degree of the Gr. CL sample was the highest of the recycled materials: 86 % Pristine >82 % Gr. CL > 67 % Gr. LC (calculated from the d-spacing of each sample on the (002) crystal plane), which is in accordance with the crystallite size values (a decrease in crystallite size values relates to lower graphitization degrees) [19]. The lower graphitization degree of the Gr.LC samples is ascribed to the exfoliation caused by the leaching solvents in the graphite when solvent molecules are inserted into the graphene layers [19,42]. This reduces the crystallite stacking (Lc) and causes breakage of the layers reducing, their width (La). By performing the calcination before the leaching, the material is protected by the regeneration of the graphene layer stacking and, thus, is less reactive during the leaching. The graphitization degree for GrC.CL was also calculated and exceeded the value of Gr. CL by 2 %, with unit size parameters more similar to pristine graphite than the recycled materials from production scraps, successfully proving the validity of the process for both non-cycled and cycled electrodes.

For a complete material validation, electrochemical tests were performed in half-coin cells with lithium metal counter electrode (Fig. 9 ). Discharge capacity obtained for pristine, Gr.LC, Gr.CL and GrC.CL materials are 363.0 ± 0.4, 337.9 ± 1.7, 354.5 ± 0.9 and 357.1 ± 1.7 mAh/gAM, respectively. The initial coulombic efficiency is 10.20 % for cells with pristine graphite electrodes and 10.6 %, 10.1 and 11.5 % for cells with Gr.LC, Gr.CL and GrC.CL, respectively. Although a lower copper content was observed in EDX analysis (Table 2), galvanostatic profiles are very similar indicating that the recycling process was effective, with no side-reactions due to the presence of copper or other contaminations. However, discharge capacities are slightly lower for recycled samples, indicating a possible graphite modification during acid leaching (in agreement with the change of graphitization degree observed in XRD), or revealing possible traces of the carbon additive used in previous electrodes. However, the graphite capacity is recovered up to more than 90 %, and its reuse in Li-ion cells is possible, validating the direct recycling process.

The validation and optimization of a direct recycling protocol for graphite electrodes, coming from production scraps and EoL electrodes, was targeted to establish a sustainable and efficient process based on the industry standards to obtain recycled graphite with regenerated structure and no polymeric compounds. On a first trial, a reference direct recycling protocol was tested with production scraps, proving that the process based on a mild leaching and calcination step successfully eliminated the binders and copper from the current collector and regenerated graphite could be obtained. The order of the direct recyling steps was determined as non-crucial for production scraps in the final characteristics of the recovered material. A DOE was then proposed to determine the optimum leaching parameters combination: acid concentration, solid-to-liquid ratio, and leaching time and temperature. By analysing the obtained graphite samples two conditions were selected and, along with an LCA study, the following parameters were determined to be the most sustainable and efficient based on the recovered material characteristics and process sustainability: 1.2 M H2SO4 + 5v.% H2O2, at 40 °C for 20 min with a solid-to-liquid ratio of 65 g L−1. The direct recycling process was validated with production scraps and EoL electrodes, and the order of the steps was established as: i) mild calcination, ii) mild leaching, as it is more convenient to calcinate the cycled electrodes first to avoid potentially dangerous reactions of the lithium traces. TGA and XRD results confirmed that no polymeric compounds remained on the final materials and the structure of the graphite was successfully restored with high recovery yields (>85 %). The electrochemical testing has also proved the suitability of the material to be reinserted in the electrode processing loop with specific capacities above 90 % of the pristine material.

This work has established a direct recycling route for graphite anodes that improves the sustainability and environmental impact of the currently employed standard procedures, reducing the cost and creating a closed loop. Besides sulfuric acid, eco-friendly organic acids (such as citric acid, formic acid, oxalic acid) are emerging in the recycling industry and its impact can be evaluated in a future work. The recovered materials present promising structural and morphological characteristics that will be further characterized and electrochemically tested in future research.

Ane Muguruza-Sánchez: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Susan Sananes-Israel: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Enrique Moliner: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Edgar Contreras: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Imanol Landa-Medrano: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Verónica Palomares: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Iratxe de Meatza: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

P. acknowledges the support of Gobierno Vasco/Eusko Jaurlaritza (project IT1546-22). The TGA characterizations were performed in Laboratorio Naturklima/Naturklima Laborategia from CIDETEC, funded by Naturklima and Diputación Foral de Gipuzkoa.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
figs1
FE-SEM image of a pristine graphite sample with x1000 magnification.
figs1
figs2
EDX images of graphite samples recycled with different leaching parameters, selected by applying a DOE. Red regions represent copper traces present in the material.
figs2
figs3
Representative samples in which the copper was not eliminated (left) and was eliminated (right) during the leaching.
figs3
figs4
XRD spectra and fitting of the commercial untreated graphite and the recycled materials from scrap and EoL electrodes following a calcination and a leaching (CL).
